title: Docker
objectives: Use compose for local multi-service dev

sections.markdown: Docker packages an app and its dependencies into a portable **image** that runs as an isolated **container** using Linux namespaces + cgroups. It solves 'works on my machine' by making the environment part of the artifact.

rows: A running instance of an image

sections.code:
# Multi-stage: build small, run smaller
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jdk AS build
WORKDIR /src
COPY . .
RUN ./gradlew bootJar

FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=build /src/build/libs/*.jar app.jar
USER 1000
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["java","-jar","/app/app.jar"]

sections.markdown: **Layer caching** = Dockerfile performance. Docker caches each instruction as a layer; changing an earlier line invalidates all following. Copy `pom.xml`/`package.json` and install deps BEFORE copying source, so a code change doesn't reinstall the world.
Running as root inside containers, or shipping a 1GB image because you baked JDK+build tools into the runtime layer. Use multi-stage builds + a small base (alpine/distroless) + a non-root USER.

Pin base images by tag/digest, add `.dockerignore` (skip .git, node_modules), define a HEALTHCHECK, and scan images (Trivy/Grype) in CI. Never bake secrets into images — use env or a secret manager at runtime.

flashcards.back: Image = read-only template; container = a running instance of that image.
Build in a big image with all tools; copy just the artifact into a tiny runtime image.
Running as root, huge base images, secrets baked in, unscanned images.

quiz.explain: Containers share the host kernel with namespace/cgroup isolation.
You leave build tools behind and only ship the artifact.

quiz.q: The right place for production secrets is…

challenges.text: Write a Dockerfile for a Spring Boot app; build and run it.
Rewrite it as a multi-stage build using a distroless runtime image.
Set up docker-compose with app + Postgres + Redis for local dev.
Add HEALTHCHECK, non-root USER, image scanning (Trivy) in CI.

interviewQuestions: What is layer caching and how do you exploit it?
How do you keep images small and secure?
